After his arrest, Patrick was held on a $10,000 cash bond. He faced two felony counts of third-degree assault for pointing the rifle at officers, one felony count of second-degree terroristic threat for threatening to kill himself, one misdemeanor count of an adult possessing a weapon on school property, one misdemeanor count of failure to secure his weapon, and one misdemeanor count of disorderly conduct. In short, Patrick faced up to 16 and a half years if found guilty and given the maximum sentence. Three months later, Patrick took a plea deal, pleading guilty to third-degree assault and possessing a weapon at a school. Court records show he received little to no jail time and was placed on probation. Less than six weeks later, he violated that probation. His failure to comply with court proceedings would eventually lead to a second arrest and a warrant for a third arrest. On December 23rd, 2022, State Troopers again attempted to apprehend him. This time, Patrick allegedly concealed a firearm and according to reports, began to manipulate the rifle.
You all good?
Is there anyone else?

Troopers Drew Massey and Giovanni Summers opened fire. It appears officials did not perform or at least continue to perform CPR on Patrick until 11 minutes after troopers announced they secured the rifle. No body cam footage is available as Alaska State Police were not issued them in 2022. Reports indicate 20-year-old Patrick was later pronounced deceased at the hospital. According to the report by the Alaska Department of Public Safety, this was one of the 18 deaths from an officer-involved shooting in Alaska in a five-year span.
